summary: Das, Draganić, and Steiner (arXiv:2111.05723, 2021) proved that for any subcubic $n$-vertex graph $H$ and integer $q \geq 2$, the Alon–Krivelevich function satisfies $f(H,q) \leq \frac{21}{2}qn + 8n + 14q$, which is linear in $q$ and optimal up to a constant factor. Since cycles are subcubic, this directly establishes $g(q) = O(q)$, confirming Conjecture (linearity of $g(q)$) from arXiv:2012.05112 and removing the $\log q$ factor that Alon and Krivelevich attributed to the probabilistic method. The paper was subsequently published in the Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B (2024).

since_posted.title: Tight bounds for divisible subdivisions
since_posted.authors: Shagnik Das, Nemanja Draganić, Raphael Steiner
since_posted.venue: Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B
since_posted.claim: Proves f(H,q) ≤ (21/2)qn + 8n + 14q for all subcubic H, establishing g(q) = O(q) and confirming the conjecture that the O(q log q) bound is not tight.

since_posted.title: Divisible subdivisions of graphs in subdivisions of complete graphs
since_posted.claim: Improves the linear bounds of Das–Draganić–Steiner for specific cases (trees, cycles, 5-degenerate graphs) and works toward the exact value s_q(H) = m(q-1)+n.

search_queries: "divisible subdivisions" graph theory linear g(q) Alon Krivelevich
notes: The conjecture is resolved by arXiv:2111.05723. The key insight is that a subdivision of C_3 with all edge-paths of length divisible by q yields a cycle of length divisible by q, so f(C_3,q) = O(q) implies g(q) = O(q). The 2025 follow-up (arXiv:2510.05697) refines the constant factors and addresses exact values in special cases.
